name: auth
context: Authentication methods for the GitHub API: PATs, GitHub Apps, OAuth
info: Authentication is required for all GitHub API interactions. This guide covers the four primary methods: Personal Access Tokens (classic and fine-grained) for simple user-scoped access, GitHub Apps for machine-to-machine automation with fine-grained permissions, OAuth Apps for third-party user authorization, and fine-grained tokens for repository-scoped access. Each method has different rate limits, permission models, and use cases. Agents typically use PATs for prototyping and GitHub Apps for production. All skills in this ecosystem â€” [[github/repos]], [[github/issues]], [[github/pulls]], [[github/actions]], [[github/webhooks]] â€” require one of these authentication methods.

sections.title: Personal Access Tokens
sections.body: Classic PATs provide broad scope-based access (`repo`, `workflow`, `admin:org`). Generate from GitHub Settings > Developer settings > Personal access tokens > Tokens (classic). Set as `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable. Used by all skills: [[github/repos]], [[github/issues]], [[github/pulls]], [[github/actions]], [[github/webhooks]].

sections.title: GitHub Apps
sections.body: GitHub Apps authenticate as installations with fine-grained permissions. Use `@octokit/auth-app` with `appId`, `privateKey`, and `installationId`. Apps have their own identity and don't consume a user seat. Preferred for production bots, CI/CD integrations, and organizational automation. Rate limit: 5,000 requests/hour per installation.

sections.title: OAuth flow
sections.body: OAuth Apps redirect users to GitHub for authorization, exchange an authorization code for an access token, then make API calls on behalf of the user. Requires `client_id` and `client_secret`. Use for third-party web applications that need user-authorized access to GitHub resources.

sections.title: Fine-grained tokens
sections.body: Fine-grained PATs provide repository-level and permission-level access control. Select specific repositories and individual permissions (e.g., Contents: read, Issues: write). Require expiration dates. Organizations can require approval for fine-grained tokens. Preferred over classic PATs for scoped automation.

links.context: Actions API and secrets require authentication
Token needs repo scope to create repositories
Token needs issues permission to create issues
Token needs pull request permission to open PRs
Token needs admin scope to manage Actions secrets
Token needs admin:repo_hook scope to manage webhooks

related.note: All repo operations depend on proper authentication
Actions workflows get an automatic GITHUB_TOKEN with limited permissions
Webhook secrets are a separate credential from API auth tokens

author: dojo-community
